questions.q: The idèle group $\mathbb{A}_K^\times$ of a number field $K$ is defined as:
choices: the direct product $\prod_v K_v^\times$ over all places
the restricted product $\prod'_v K_v^\times$, with $x_v\in\mathcal{O}_v^\times$ for almost all finite $v$
the completion of $K^\times$ at a single prime
questions.explain: The idèle group is the restricted product of the local multiplicative groups $K_v^\times$ with respect to the compact subgroups $\mathcal{O}_v^\times$ — almost every component must be a local unit.
By definition $C_K = \mathbb{A}_K^\times / K^\times$, the quotient of the idèles by the diagonally embedded global units $K^\times$.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, the class number is $h_K=2$. What is the order of $\operatorname{Cl}(K)$?
questions.explain: The order of the ideal class group is exactly the class number, so $|\operatorname{Cl}(K)|=h_K=2$.

hard.q: Let $\alpha=(\alpha_v)\in\mathbb{A}_K^\times$. The idèle $\alpha$ represents a principal idèle (i.e., lies in the image of $K^\times\hookrightarrow\mathbb{A}_K^\times$) if and only if:
choices: $\alpha_v=\alpha_w$ for all pairs of finite places $v,w$
there exists $x\in K^\times$ with $\alpha_v=x$ for all places $v$
hard.hint: Principal ideles are diagonal embeddings of elements of $K^\times$.
hard.explain: The diagonal embedding sends $x\in K^\times$ to the idèle with every component equal to $x$. The last condition is merely the restricted-product condition satisfied by every idèle, not a characterisation of principal idèles.

hard.q: Which statement about $C_K=\mathbb{A}_K^\times/K^\times$ is FALSE for a number field $K$?
choices: There is a surjective homomorphism $C_K\to\operatorname{Cl}(K)$ with compact kernel
The connected component $C_K^0$ of the identity in $C_K$ is compact
hard.hint: One statement claims something false about connected components.
hard.explain: $C_K$ is locally compact but NOT compact — it surjects onto $\mathbb{R}_{>0}$ via the idèlic norm $\|\cdot\|$. Only the norm-1 subgroup $C_K^1=\ker\|\cdot\|$ is compact (Fujisaki's lemma), and that compactness encodes the unit theorem and finiteness of class number.

hard.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-23})$ the class number is $h_K=3$. The Hilbert class field $H/K$ has degree $[H:K]=h_K$. What is $[H:\mathbb{Q}]$?

questions.q: The Hilbert class field $H$ of a number field $K$ is characterised as:
choices: the smallest extension of $K$ in which every prime splits completely
the maximal abelian extension of $K$ unramified at every finite and infinite place
the maximal totally ramified abelian extension of $K$
the compositum of all cyclotomic extensions of $K$
questions.explain: By definition $H$ is the maximal abelian extension of $K$ in which every prime (finite and infinite) is unramified. The Artin map then gives $\operatorname{Gal}(H/K)\cong\operatorname{Cl}(K)$.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-14})$, which has class number $h_K=4$, what is the degree $[H:K]$ of the Hilbert class field over $K$?
questions.explain: The Artin isomorphism $\operatorname{Gal}(H/K)\cong\operatorname{Cl}(K)$ forces $[H:K]=|\operatorname{Cl}(K)|=h_K$. Here $h_K=4$, so $[H:K]=4$.

questions.q: The principal ideal theorem says that for any ideal $\mathfrak{a}\subset\mathcal{O}_K$:
questions.explain: Furtwängler's principal ideal theorem: every ideal of $\mathcal{O}_K$ becomes principal when lifted to $\mathcal{O}_H$. Ideal classes of $K$ capitulate in the Hilbert class field.

hard.q: An odd rational prime $p$ splits completely in the Hilbert class field $H$ of $K$ if and only if:
choices: $(p)$ is principal in $\mathcal{O}_K$ (as an ideal of $K$)
the Frobenius at $p$ in $\operatorname{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})$ is trivial
hard.hint: Complete splitting in $H/K$ means the Frobenius lies in the identity class.
hard.explain: By Artin reciprocity, $\operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}\in\operatorname{Gal}(H/K)\cong\operatorname{Cl}(K)$ is trivial iff the ideal class of $\mathfrak{P}\mid p$ is the identity, i.e., $\mathfrak{P}$ (and hence $(p)$) is principal. Thus $p$ splits completely in $H/K$ iff every prime of $K$ above $p$ is principal.

hard.q: The class field tower of $K$ is the sequence $K=K_0\subset K_1\subset K_2\subset\cdots$ where $K_{n+1}$ is the Hilbert class field of $K_n$. This tower is known to be:
choices: always finite, terminating when some $K_n$ has $h_{K_n}=1$
sometimes infinite (Golod–Shafarevich) and sometimes finite, depending on $K$
hard.hint: Golod-Shafarevich gave infinite towers; finite termination is the open question historically resolved negatively.
hard.explain: Golod–Shafarevich (1964) produced the first examples of infinite class field towers (e.g., $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3\cdot5\cdot7\cdot11\cdot13\cdot17})$). But many fields, like $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, have finite towers terminating at the first Hilbert class field.

hard.q: Let $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, $h_K=2$, $H=$ Hilbert class field of $K$, $h_H=1$. How many steps does the class field tower take before reaching a field with class number $1$?
hard.hint: If $h_H = 1$, the tower stops immediately at $H$.
hard.explain: The tower is $K\subset H$ with $h_H=1$, so $H_1=H$ already has trivial class group. The tower stabilises after $1$ step.

questions.q: For the cyclotomic extension $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}$, the Artin map sends an unramified prime $p\nmid n$ to the Galois element:
questions.explain: Cyclotomic reciprocity: $\operatorname{Frob}_p(\zeta_n)=\zeta_n^p$ for $p\nmid n$. This identifies $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q})$ with $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$ via $\sigma_p\leftrightarrow p\bmod n$.

questions.q: Take $L=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{12})/\mathbb{Q}$, so $\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/12)^\times$. The Frobenius at $p=13$ corresponds to $13\bmod 12$. Enter that residue.
questions.explain: $13 = 12+1$, so $13\equiv 1\pmod{12}$. The Frobenius at $p=13$ is the identity, reflecting that $13$ splits completely in $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{12})$.

questions.q: For a finite abelian $L/K$ of conductor $\mathfrak{m}$, Artin reciprocity asserts that the Artin map induces a surjection
choices: $\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K)\twoheadrightarrow\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$ whose kernel is generated by primes that split completely in $L$
questions.explain: The main theorem: the Artin map factors through the ray class group $\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K)$ and is surjective onto $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$, with kernel generated by the classes of primes splitting completely in $L$.

hard.q:
Put the following steps of the proof that the Artin map is well-defined on ideal classes in the correct logical order.
(I) Show $\operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{p}$ depends only on the ideal $(\pi)$, not on the choice of uniformiser $\pi$.
(II) Extend the Frobenius symbol multiplicatively to fractional ideals coprime to $\mathfrak{m}$.
(III) Verify that principal ideals $(\alpha)$ with $\alpha\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}}$ lie in the kernel (the reciprocity law).
(IV) Conclude the map descends to $\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K)$.
hard.hint: Unramified primes come first; then principal ideals; finally invariance on classes.
hard.explain: First pin down $\operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{p}$ intrinsically (I), then extend to all fractional ideals by multiplicativity (II), then prove the key reciprocity kernel claim (III), and finally descend to the ray class group (IV).

hard.q: In $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_7)/\mathbb{Q}$, the prime $p=2$ is unramified (since $2\nmid 7$). The Frobenius $\operatorname{Frob}_2$ acts as $\zeta_7\mapsto\zeta_7^2$, so $\operatorname{Frob}_2$ has order equal to the multiplicative order of $2$ modulo $7$. What is that order?
hard.explain: $2^1=2,\; 2^2=4,\; 2^3=8\equiv 1\pmod 7$. So $\operatorname{ord}_7(2)=3$, meaning $p=2$ splits into $\varphi(7)/3=2$ primes in $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_7)$.

hard.q: Which is a correct counterexample showing the Artin map need NOT be injective as a map from fractional ideals (before passing to the class group)?
choices: Any principal ideal $(\alpha)$ with $\alpha\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}}$ maps to the identity in $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$, yet $(\alpha)$ may be non-trivial as a fractional ideal.
The Artin map is always injective on fractional ideals.
The Frobenius symbol is only defined for prime ideals, so the map never extends to fractional ideals.
Principal ideals always have trivial Frobenius regardless of the conductor.
hard.hint: Distinct primes of the same residue class can share a Frobenius.
hard.explain: The Artin map kills the subgroup of principal ideals $\{(\alpha):\alpha\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}}\}$, which is non-trivial whenever there are units or non-trivial principal ideals satisfying the congruence. Injectivity on the quotient (the ray class group) is what the theory gives.

local-global-cft.title: Local and global class field theory

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}$ and modulus $\mathfrak{m}=(n)\cdot\infty$, the ray class field $K(\mathfrak{m})$ equals:
questions.explain: Kronecker–Weber in class-field-theoretic form: the ray class field of $\mathbb{Q}$ with conductor $n\cdot\infty$ is exactly $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$, and $\operatorname{Cl}_{n\infty}(\mathbb{Q})=(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times\cong\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q})$.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}$, the order of the ray class group $\operatorname{Cl}_{12\infty}(\mathbb{Q})=(\mathbb{Z}/12)^\times$ is:
The idelic Artin reciprocity map $\theta_K:C_K\twoheadrightarrow\operatorname{Gal}(K^{\mathrm{ab}}/K)$ has:
choices: kernel equal to the connected component of the identity in $C_K$
questions.explain: The kernel of the global reciprocity map is exactly the connected component of $1$ in $C_K$; finite abelian extensions $L/K$ correspond to finite-index open subgroups of $C_K$ containing this connected component.

hard.q: Local class field theory gives an Artin map $\theta_v: K_v^\times \to \operatorname{Gal}(K_v^{\mathrm{ab}}/K_v)$. For an unramified local extension $L_v/K_v$, the map $\theta_v$ sends a uniformiser $\pi_v$ to:
choices: the arithmetic Frobenius $\operatorname{Frob}_v$ (the canonical generator of $\operatorname{Gal}(L_v/K_v)$)
an element of order $2$ regardless of the residue field
hard.hint: Unramified uniformizers land on Frobenius under the local Artin map.
hard.explain: By local reciprocity, $\theta_v(\pi_v)=\operatorname{Frob}_v$, the arithmetic Frobenius ($x\mapsto x^{|\mathbf{k}_v|}$ on residue fields). The geometric Frobenius convention differs by inversion and appears in $\ell$-adic cohomology.

hard.q: The local-global compatibility of Artin maps states that for each place $v$ of $K$ there is a commutative square. Which arrow correctly describes the local-to-global map on the Galois side?
choices: The restriction $\operatorname{Gal}(K^{\mathrm{ab}}/K)\to\operatorname{Gal}(K_v^{\mathrm{ab}}/K_v)$ given by choosing a decomposition group
hard.hint: The global Artin map restricts to local ones via the place-$v$ inclusion.
hard.explain: Fixing an embedding $\bar{K}\hookrightarrow\bar{K}_v$ determines a decomposition group $D_v\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Gal}(K^{\mathrm{ab}}/K)$, and its image in the maximal abelian quotient maps to $\operatorname{Gal}(K_v^{\mathrm{ab}}/K_v)$. The local Artin map for $K_v^\times$ then commutes with the global one via the inclusion $K_v^\times\hookrightarrow\mathbb{A}_K^\times$.

hard.q: The ray class group $\operatorname{Cl}_{8\infty}(\mathbb{Q})=(\mathbb{Z}/8)^\times$. Compute its order $\varphi(8)$.

questions.q: Takagi's existence theorem (idelic form) states that finite abelian extensions $L/K$ correspond bijectively to:
questions.explain: Every finite-index open subgroup $N\subset C_K$ is the kernel of the Artin map for a unique finite abelian extension $L/K$, and vice versa. This is the class-field bijection.

questions.q: Under the bijection $L\leftrightarrow N_L\subset C_K$, the inclusion of abelian extensions $L\subseteq L'$ corresponds to:
questions.explain: The correspondence is order-reversing: a bigger field $L'$ has a smaller kernel $N_{L'}$. Equivalently, $L=(K^{\mathrm{ab}})^{N_L}$, so inclusions of fields flip to reverse inclusions of subgroups.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}$, Kronecker–Weber plus Takagi existence says every finite abelian $L/\mathbb{Q}$ sits in some $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$. Take $L=\mathbb{Q}(i,\sqrt{5})$. The smallest $n$ for which $L\subseteq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ is $n = ?$
questions.explain: The conductor of $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ is $4$ and of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$ is $5$; by the conductor-discriminant/Takagi existence correspondence the conductor of their compositum is $\operatorname{lcm}(4,5)=20$, so $\mathbb{Q}(i,\sqrt{5})\subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{20})$ and no smaller $n$ works.

hard.q:
Arrange the key steps of Takagi's existence theorem proof in logical order.
(I) Use the norm-index inequality $[G:N_{L/K}(C_L)]\le[L:K]$ from class field axioms.
(II) Show the Artin map is surjective onto $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$ via the splitting density argument.
(III) Combine steps (I) and (II) to conclude the Artin map is an isomorphism $C_K/N_{L/K}(C_L)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$.
(IV) Deduce every finite-index open $N\subset C_K$ arises as a norm group.
hard.hint: Inequality first, reciprocity second, then surjectivity onto the open subgroup.
hard.explain: The norm-index inequality (I) bounds the index from above; surjectivity (II) implies the index equals $[L:K]$; together they force the Artin isomorphism (III); then one constructs the required extensions for all open subgroups (IV).

hard.q: Which statement is a correct consequence of Takagi's existence theorem but would be FALSE without it?
choices: Every abelian extension $L/K$ has a conductor.
For every finite-index open $N\subset C_K$ there exists a finite abelian extension $L/K$ with $N=N_{L/K}(C_L)$.
The Artin map $C_K\to\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$ is a group homomorphism.
The Frobenius element at a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ is well-defined up to conjugacy.
hard.hint: Existence gives the converse: every suitable subgroup comes from an abelian extension.
hard.explain: The hard half of the existence theorem is precisely the surjectivity: every open finite-index subgroup is a norm group, i.e., every such $N$ arises as $N_{L/K}(C_L)$ for some $L$. The other statements are either formal or do not require this surjectivity.

hard.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}$, the abelian extension $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})$ has Galois group $\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$. The conductors of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3})$, and $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{6})$ are $8$, $12$, and $24$ respectively. The conductor of $L$ is $\operatorname{lcm}(8,12)$. Compute that value.
hard.hint: Its Galois group is a product of two copies of $\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$.
hard.explain: $\operatorname{lcm}(8,12)=24$. Thus $L\subseteq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{24})$ and the conductor of $L/\mathbb{Q}$ is $24$.

questions.q: The conductor-discriminant formula expresses the discriminant of a finite abelian extension $L/K$ as:
questions.explain: Hasse's Führerdiskriminantenproduktformel: the relative discriminant of an abelian $L/K$ factors as the product of the Artin conductors of its 1-dimensional characters.

questions.q: For $L=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q}$, the four characters of $\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/5)^\times$ have conductors $1,5,5,5$ (the trivial character has conductor $1$; the three non-trivial characters all have conductor $5$). Compute the absolute discriminant $|\operatorname{disc}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5))|=1\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 5$.
questions.explain: By the conductor-discriminant formula $|\operatorname{disc}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5))|=\prod_\chi \mathfrak{f}(\chi)=1\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 5 = 125$, matching the classical value $\operatorname{disc}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5))=5^3$.

questions.q: A prime $\mathfrak{p}$ of $K$ is unramified in the abelian extension $L/K$ if and only if:
questions.explain: A prime ramifies in $L$ iff some character $\chi$ of $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$ is non-trivial on its inertia subgroup, iff $\mathfrak{p}\mid\mathfrak{f}(\chi)$ for some non-trivial $\chi$. Unramified primes appear in no conductor and hence contribute nothing to the discriminant.

hard.q: For $L=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_8)/\mathbb{Q}$, we have $\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/8)^\times\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$, with four characters each of conductor dividing $8$. The trivial character has conductor $1$; the three non-trivial characters each have conductor $8$. Compute $|\operatorname{disc}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_8))|=1\cdot 8\cdot 8\cdot 8$.
hard.hint: For $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ the conductors of the three nontrivial characters add up.

hard.q: Let $L/K$ be abelian with $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)\cong\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ for a prime $p$. The conductor-discriminant formula gives $\mathfrak{d}_{L/K}=\prod_{\chi\neq\chi_0}\mathfrak{f}(\chi)$. If all non-trivial characters share the same conductor $\mathfrak{f}$, how many non-trivial characters are there?
hard.hint: For a cyclic degree-$p$ extension the discriminant exponent is $p-1$ times the conductor.
hard.explain: $|\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)|=p$, so its character group also has order $p$; subtracting the trivial character leaves $p-1$ non-trivial characters. The discriminant is $\mathfrak{f}^{p-1}$.

hard.q:
Which is a correct chain of implications linking the conductor-discriminant formula back to the Artin map?
(I) Unramified $\mathfrak{p}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\mathfrak{p}\nmid\mathfrak{f}(\chi)$ for all non-trivial $\chi$.
(II) $\mathfrak{p}\nmid\mathfrak{d}_{L/K}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $\mathfrak{p}$ unramified in $L/K$.
(III) The Artin map is well-defined at $\mathfrak{p}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $\mathfrak{p}$ unramified.
choices: III $\Rightarrow$ I $\Rightarrow$ II (and their converses tie together)
I $\Rightarrow$ II $\Rightarrow$ III, forming a chain linking character conductors to the Artin map
II is independent of I and III
I and III are equivalent but II is unrelated
hard.hint: Unramifiedness of a character is exactly trivial inertia action.
hard.explain: The standard chain: (I) gives that unramified primes avoid all character conductors; (II) rephrases this via the product formula for the discriminant; (III) notes the Frobenius/Artin symbol is only defined at unramified primes. The implication flows I $\Rightarrow$ II $\Rightarrow$ III with matching converses.
